Começar sessão

Tutor: Danilo, aqui vai uma situação pra você pensar:

Você recebe uma mensagem dizendo "transferência de R$10.000 autorizada por Danilo." Como você prova que foi *você* que mandou isso — e não alguém se passando por você?

Student: por meio de uma assinatira digital que possui um hash criptografado usada minha chave privada única. uso minha chave publica para verificar se o hash descriptgrafado é de fato meu hash (que repreenta minha identidade)

Tutor: Boa intuição — você chegou perto do mecanismo real. Mas tem um detalhe importante na sua descrição que vale explorar.

Você disse que a chave pública "descriptografa o hash pra verificar que é seu." O que exatamente está sendo comparado nessa verificação? O que o verificador tem de um lado, e o que ele tem do outro?

Student: o comparcao sera: o meu hash original, com o hsah gerado a partir do hash da transacao descriptografado usando minha chave publica

Tutor: Quase — mas tem uma confusão pequena aí que faz diferença.

Você mencionou "hash da transação descriptografado." Mas espera: hash tem descriptografia?

Student: sim. se ele for criptografado usando uma chave privada e depois descriptografado com a publica, nesse caso ele tem sim. mas claro que ele continua sendo hash

Tutor: Certo, você está descrevendo a operação de criptografia assimétrica aplicada ao hash — isso está correto.

Então me diz: por que assinar o *hash* da mensagem, em vez de assinar a mensagem inteira diretamente?

Student: para ser mais barato. mensagem inteira é muito cara

Tutor: Exato. Agora junta tudo isso num fluxo completo.

Quem assina, o quê, com qual chave — e quem verifica, o quê, com qual chave. Me descreve o processo do começo ao fim, em ordem.

Student: eu assino com minha chave privada. eu verifico com minha chave publica

Tutor: Certo, mas me dá o fluxo completo — o que acontece com a mensagem antes de você assinar? E o que o verificador faz com a assinatura antes de comparar?

Student: mensagem vira hash -> eu assino c privada (criptografa) -> verificador usa publica para descriptografar -> bate os dois hashes e verifica se há integridade -> se houver -> verificada com sucesso que é autentica

Tutor: Perfeito. Esse é exatamente o fluxo correto.

Uma pergunta agora: esse processo garante *autenticidade* — mas garante *confidencialidade*? A mensagem em si fica protegida de terceiros?

Student: Nao. a mensagem pode ser descriptografada por qualquer um usanda minha chave publica

Tutor: Exato. Assinatura digital garante autenticidade e integridade, mas não sigilo.

Agora vamos para o próximo conceito, que usa algo que você já conhece. Imagina que você é um minerador de Bitcoin e alguém te diz: "pra adicionar o próximo bloco na cadeia, você precisa encontrar um número que, quando combinado com os dados do bloco e passado por uma função hash, produza um resultado começando com dez zeros."

Como você encontraria esse número?

Student: testaria n combinacoes ate a funcao hash produzir um hash que atenda ao criterio

Tutor: Exato. E por que isso é difícil — sendo que verificar se a solução está certa é trivial?

Student: sim

Tutor: Espera, não entendi sua resposta — você quis confirmar que é difícil, ou quis explicar o porquê?

Me diz: o que torna *encontrar* o número difícil, sendo que *verificar* é tão simples?

Student: a quantidade de vezes que é enorme - cara

Tutor: Exato — e essa assimetria é justamente o ponto central do Proof of Work.

Encontrar custa muito (tentativas, energia, tempo). Verificar custa quase nada (uma única passada pelo hash).

Agora me diz: por que essa assimetria é útil num sistema como o Bitcoin, onde ninguém confia em ninguém?

Student: pois isso dificulta o processo de validacao. se validar fosse facil, a rede poderia ser facilmente fraudada usando força bruta. a assimetria é o que faz com que nao existam padroes ou atalhos para encontrar a noonce

Tutor: Quase — mas tem uma inversão sutil aí. Você disse que a assimetria dificulta a *validação*. Mas validar é fácil, lembra? O que ela dificulta de verdade é outra coisa.

Pensa assim: se um atacante quisesse reescrever o histórico de transações do Bitcoin, o que ele precisaria fazer?

Student: encontrar o nooce de todos os blocos - praticamente impossivel

Tutor: Exato. A assimetria dificulta *fraudar*, não validar.

E tem um custo concreto embutido nisso — eletricidade, hardware, tempo. Por que esse custo econômico importa para a segurança da rede?

Student: pois esse custo faz com que a fraude nao valha a pena economicamente

Tutor: Perfeito. Você acabou de fechar o raciocínio central do Proof of Work.

Agora vamos para mineração — que é esse processo em detalhes. Me diz primeiro: na sua cabeça, o que um minerador está fazendo quando "minera" um bloco? Descreve o passo a passo.

Student: ele esta encontrando o noonce que da acesso aos dados do bloco e com esses dados, criar o bloco seguinte

Tutor: Quase — mas tem uma confusão aí. O nonce não "dá acesso" aos dados do bloco. Os dados já estão lá, abertos.

O que o nonce realmente faz dentro do processo de criação do bloco?

Student: concede os direitos sobre o bloco

Tutor: Não exatamente. Vou te dar um passo atrás.

O nonce é só um número. Um número que o minerador testa, testa, testa. Quando ele combina esse número com os dados do bloco e passa pelo hash, o que ele está tentando obter?

Student: o hash do bloco

Tutor: Certo — mas não qualquer hash. O que esse hash precisa ter de especial pra ser aceito pela rede?

Student: precisa ter um determinado numero de zeros

Tutor: Exato. Então o nonce não "concede direitos" — ele é só uma variável que o minerador ajusta até o hash do bloco satisfazer essa condição.

Agora me diz: quando um minerador encontra o nonce certo e divulga o bloco pra rede, o que os outros nós fazem antes de aceitar esse bloco?

Student: validam se o que o minerador enconrou procede

Tutor: Certo. E como eles validam? O que eles calculam pra confirmar?

Student: o noonce

Tutor: Quase — eles não precisam encontrar o nonce de novo. O minerador já entregou o nonce pra eles.

O que eles fazem com esse nonce que o minerador forneceu?

Student: aplciam esse mesmo noonce para ver se obtem o resultado correto

Tutor: Exato. Eles pegam o nonce, combinam com os dados do bloco, calculam o hash — e verificam se começa com os zeros exigidos. Uma operação só.

Agora a pergunta que fecha o ciclo: o minerador que encontrou o nonce recebe alguma recompensa. Por que essa recompensa existe no design do Bitcoin?

Student: sim. essa recompensa incentiva os mineradores a continuarem minerando

Tutor: Certo — mas vai um nível mais fundo. O que aconteceria com a segurança da rede se não houvesse recompensa?

Student: seria comprometida pois o incentivo diminui o numero de desonetos. compensa mais ser honesto do que desonesto - teoria dos jogos

Tutor: Perfeito. Você conectou mineração, Proof of Work e teoria dos jogos num raciocínio só — e foi você que trouxe essa conexão, não eu.

Agora chegamos no Bitcoin como estudo de caso. Pensa nessa situação: uma exchange quer usar blockchain pra registrar transações entre usuários. O CTO propõe usar uma blockchain privada, sem mineração, sem Proof of Work. Um engenheiro discorda e diz que precisam do Bitcoin mesmo.

Quem você apoiaria — e por quê?

Student: um engenherio. na privada sem proof of work, a desonestidade compensa. garnde chance de fralde

Tutor: Interessante — mas pensa um pouco mais. Numa blockchain privada, quem são os participantes? São anônimos como no Bitcoin, ou são identidades conhecidas?

Student: identidades conhecidas. entao o CTO esta certo. nao tem necessidade de pow. sera apenas mais caro. as entidades de auditacao da privada sao suficiente para garantir veracidade

Tutor: Exato. Você virou a resposta sozinho quando trouxe a informação certa.

Isso é o núcleo da decisão: PoW existe pra criar confiança entre desconhecidos. Quando os participantes são conhecidos e responsabilizáveis, você não precisa do custo computacional — outros mecanismos de consenso são suficientes.

Última pergunta da sessão: olhando tudo que a gente cobriu hoje — assinaturas digitais, Proof of Work, mineração, Bitcoin — o que você conectaria que no começo da sessão parecia separado?

Student: assinaturas digitais e mineracao

Tutor: O que as conecta, na sua visão?

Student: principio da assimetria

Tutor: Desenvolve isso — assimetria em qual sentido em cada uma?

Student: a mesma assietria que é usada na assinatura digital para garantir autenticidade, é usada na mineração para que muitas validacoes sejam necessarias ate enconrar o noonc

Tutor: Quase — mas são assimetrias diferentes. Na assinatura digital, a assimetria é entre chaves (privada pra assinar, pública pra verificar). Na mineração, a assimetria é entre esforço (encontrar o nonce é caro, verificar é barato).

O que elas têm em comum não é o mecanismo — é o *efeito*. Você consegue nomear esse efeito?

Student: nao determinismo

Tutor: Interessante ângulo — mas me explica o que você quer dizer com não determinismo aqui.

Student: ah sei la. chega

Tutor: Tudo bem — foi uma sessão longa e você cobriu bastante coisa.

Me diz uma coisa antes de fechar: o que foi mais difícil hoje, e por quê?

Student: antes disso me diga vc: O que elas têm em comum não é o mecanismo — é o efeito. Você consegue nomear esse efeito?

Tutor: O efeito comum é: **fácil de verificar, difícil de falsificar**.

Na assinatura digital, qualquer um verifica com a chave pública — mas só o dono da chave privada consegue assinar. Na mineração, qualquer nó verifica o nonce em um cálculo — mas encontrá-lo custou muito esforço.

Agora sim — o que foi mais difícil hoje?

Student: achei tudo facil

Tutor: Faz sentido — você chegou com bastante coisa já construída das sessões anteriores.

Uma pergunta mais honesta então: teve algum momento em que você respondeu com confiança mas não tinha tanta certeza assim?

Student: a ligacao entre assinaturas digitais e mineracao

Tutor: Faz sentido — eram dois conceitos novos e a conexão entre eles não é óbvia. Você tentou construir uma ponte e chegou perto, mesmo sem ter o vocabulário exato.

Na próxima sessão a gente entra em carteiras Bitcoin — e aí essas peças todas vão se encaixar num sistema completo. Até lá.

I will work through all four concepts in the concept graph before scoring.

---

## PHASE 1 — EVIDENCE ANALYSIS

<concept_analysis id="digital_signatures">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 5 (early):** Student's opening, unprompted response described the full mechanism — private key to sign, public key to verify, hash as the object being signed — before any scaffolding. This is first-exposure, unprompted reasoning that draws on prior asymmetric cryptography knowledge. The formulation was the student's own, albeit imprecise in one place ("hash criptografado... que representa minha identidade" suggests a mild conflation of identity-hash with message-hash).

- **Negative evidence / Weak misconception (early):** Student described the verification as comparing "o meu hash original, com o hash gerado a partir do hash da transação descriptografado." This is confused — "hash da transação descriptografado" implies the hash itself is being decrypted and then re-hashed, rather than: (a) the receiver independently hashes the message, and (b) decrypts the signature to recover the signer's hash, then compares the two. The Companion flagged it; the student defended it ("sim... mas claro que ele continua sendo hash"), which shows the confusion was not a slip but a partially incorrect mental model of what is being decrypted.

- **Level 2 (early-mid):** After the Companion pressed — "hash tem descriptografia?" — the student's clarification ("se ele for criptografado usando uma chave privada e depois descriptografado com a pública, nesse caso ele tem sim") is technically defensible when understood as asymmetric encryption applied to the hash, but arrived at through the Companion's probing question rather than independently.

- **Level 5 (mid):** "Para ser mais barato. Mensagem inteira é muito cara." — correct and independent. When asked why you sign the hash rather than the message, the student produced the efficiency rationale without scaffolding. Concise but accurate.

- **Level 3 (mid):** Full flow description: "mensagem vira hash → eu assino c privada → verificador usa pública para descriptografar → bate os dois hashes e verifica se há integridade → se houver → verificada com sucesso que é autêntica." This came after the Companion prompted for the complete flow. The student had previously stated a compressed/confused version; here the Companion explicitly requested the complete sequence, so this is scaffolded performance — but the student produced it correctly and in the right order, applying the concept procedurally.

- **Level 5 (mid-late):** "Não. A mensagem pode ser descriptografada por qualquer um usando minha chave pública." — correct, independent, and shows understanding of the distinction between authentication and confidentiality. No scaffolding preceded this; the Companion asked a direct question and the student answered accurately.

**Trajectory:** Improving. Student arrived with prior knowledge (asymmetric crypto) that let them produce a mostly-correct opening description. The weak misconception about what exactly is being "decrypted" in verification was probed and partially clarified, though not fully resolved by the student's own reasoning. Second half of the digital-signatures exchange showed consistently independent correct responses.

**Misconceptions:** Weak persisting misconception. The student's description of verification conflates the asymmetric-decryption-of-the-signature step with the hash-of-the-message step. The student described "hash da transação descriptografado" in a way that suggests the hash itself is the object decrypted and then re-checked — rather than the cleaner model: decrypt signature → recover original hash; independently hash the received message → compare. The Companion accepted the student's clarification without fully resolving this, so it remains weakly persisting. It does not reflect a fundamentally wrong model (the student knows private key signs, public key verifies, and hashes are involved), but the internal mechanics are slightly muddled.

**Assessment rationale:** Independence criterion is largely satisfied — multiple responses across the exchange were produced without scaffolding, and the confidentiality/authentication distinction was handled cleanly and independently. Trajectory was improving. The weak persisting misconception about the internal verification mechanics constrains the score modestly (cap at 0.6), but the actual score will not hit that cap given first-exposure context. Per calibration anchors, first exposure with multiple Level 5 moments and an improving trajectory places this in the 0.40–0.55 range.

</concept_analysis>

---

<concept_analysis id="proof_of_work">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 5 (early):** "Testaria n combinações até a função hash produzir um hash que atenda ao critério." — correct, independent, and produced in direct response to an open question. No scaffolding preceded this. Student correctly described brute-force nonce search on first attempt.

- **Level 1 (early):** When asked why finding the nonce is difficult but verifying is trivial, student responded "sim" — passive confirmation that did not explain anything. Companion correctly flagged this and re-asked.

- **Level 5 (mid):** "A quantidade de vezes que é enorme — cara." — accurate identification of the source of difficulty (combinatorial search space). Terse but correct, and independent.

- **Negative evidence / misconception attempt, quickly re-oriented (mid):** Student said "a assimetria dificulta o processo de validação" — inverting the correct relationship. This was a verbal slip or partial confusion: the student had correctly described the find-hard/verify-easy asymmetry immediately before, so this appears to be imprecise phrasing rather than a deep misconception. The Companion corrected it immediately.

- **Level 5 (mid):** "Encontrar o nonce de todos os blocos — praticamente impossível." — correct characterization of what an attacker would need to do. Independent, no scaffolding.

- **Level 5 (mid-late):** "Pois esse custo faz com que a fraude não valha a pena economicamente." — correct and independent. Student correctly grounded the security argument in economic incentive rather than computational impossibility alone.

**Trajectory:** Mostly flat at Level 5, with one Level 1 slip (the "sim" non-answer) and one minor inversion that the Companion corrected. The student did not need decreasing scaffolding across the session because they largely didn't need scaffolding to begin with — the concept clicked early. The inversion was corrected but the student didn't self-correct; they simply accepted the correction and moved on.

**Misconceptions:** The "assimetria dificulta a validação" inversion was corrected by the Companion. Student did not articulate why it was wrong — they just moved past it. Weak, potentially transient misconception, though not deeply resolved. Given the student's correct independent reasoning both before and after, this is likely a phrasing error rather than a structural misconception.

**Assessment rationale:** Independence criterion is strongly satisfied — multiple Level 5 moments with no scaffolding required. No generative reasoning was observed (Level 6 not present). The trajectory was flat-strong. The one inversion was corrected by the Companion without student self-correction, but surrounding evidence suggests it was a phrasing issue. Per calibration anchors, first exposure with consistent Level 5 evidence and no scaffolding required places this near the upper boundary of the first-exposure range (0.45–0.55).

</concept_analysis>

---

<concept_analysis id="mining">

Evidence moments:

- **Negative evidence / misconception (early):** "Ele está encontrando o nonce que dá acesso aos dados do bloco e com esses dados, criar o bloco seguinte." — two errors here: (1) the nonce "gives access" to block data (incorrect — data is already open), and (2) the goal is framed as creating the next block rather than satisfying a hash condition. This is a persisting functional confusion about what the nonce does.

- **Negative evidence / misconception (early, second instance):** "Concede os direitos sobre o bloco." — after the Companion clarified that the nonce doesn't give access, the student doubled down with an equally incorrect answer ("grants rights"). This shows the misconception was structural, not a slip.

- **Level 2 (mid):** "Precisa ter um determinado número de zeros." — correct, but arrived at only after the Companion walked the student through "what does the hash need?" step by step. Scaffolded performance.

- **Level 2 (mid):** "Validam se o que o minerador encontrou procede." — vague but directionally correct response to what other nodes do. Required further scaffolding.

- **Level 2 (mid):** "O nonce." — incorrect identification of what validators calculate (they don't find the nonce again, they use the provided nonce). Required correction and further scaffolding.

- **Level 2 (mid-late):** "Aplicam esse mesmo nonce para ver se obtêm o resultado correto." — finally correct after the Companion provided the scaffolding frame ("the miner already gave them the nonce"). This is Level 2 at best — the correct answer followed directly from the Companion's framing.

- **Level 5 (late):** "Seria comprometida pois o incentivo diminui o número de desonestos. Compensa mais ser honesto do que desonesto — teoria dos jogos." — strong independent response connecting mining incentives to game theory. The game-theory invocation was the student's own and was explicitly noted by the Companion as an unprompted connection.

**Trajectory:** Weak early (misconceptions about nonce function, required extensive scaffolding to reach procedural accuracy), then strong late (independent game-theoretic synthesis). The procedural mechanics of mining required scaffolding throughout; the incentive reasoning at the end was independent. This is a mixed trajectory — the conceptual/incentive layer was grasped independently, but the procedural layer required significant support.

**Misconceptions:** The nonce-as-access-key misconception was persistent across two responses early in the mining section. The Companion corrected it through scaffolding; the student did not self-correct or explain why their earlier model was wrong. The misconception appears resolved procedurally (the student eventually described the correct mechanics) but not intellectually (the student never articulated why "giving access" was the wrong frame). This is a resolved-but-not-explained misconception.

**Assessment rationale:** For the procedural layer, evidence is predominantly Level 2 with a persisting-then-corrected misconception. For the incentive/game-theory layer, evidence is Level 5 independent. Independence criterion is partially satisfied — strong on incentives, weak on procedure. The nonce-function misconception was corrected only through scaffolding, not self-correction. Per calibration anchors, first exposure with mixed Level 2 (procedural) and Level 5 (conceptual incentive) evidence places this in the 0.30–0.42 range.

</concept_analysis>

---

<concept_analysis id="bitcoin_as_case_study">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 2 (early):** "Um engenheiro. Na privada sem proof of work, a desonestidade compensa. Grande chance de fraude." — incorrect initial reasoning (private blockchains without PoW are not inherently insecure). The student imported the PoW-as-necessary-for-security framing without accounting for the identity/accountability dimension. Required one scaffolding prompt.

- **Level 4 — Self-correction at impasse (mid):** After the Companion asked a single clarifying question ("numa blockchain privada, quem são os participantes?"), the student immediately reversed their position: "Identidades conhecidas. Então o CTO está certo. Não tem necessidade de PoW. Será apenas mais caro. As entidades de auditação da privada são suficientes para garantir veracidade." This is a genuine self-correction — the student recognized their prior answer was wrong, stated the correct answer, and articulated the reasoning (known identities → PoW unnecessary → other mechanisms sufficient). The Companion confirmed this explicitly. The quality of the self-correction is moderate: it was triggered by a single scaffolding question, not fully unprompted — but the student completed the reasoning independently once the framing was provided.

**Trajectory:** Short but improving — incorrect initial reasoning, then self-corrected to accurate conclusion with one scaffolding prompt. The self-correction included substantive reasoning (not just replacing a wrong answer with a right one), which is the key Level 4 qualifier.

**Misconceptions:** Initial misconception that PoW is necessary even in permissioned/private blockchain contexts. Resolved through self-correction after one scaffolding prompt. The student articulated why PoW is unnecessary (known identities, accountability, other audit mechanisms) — this is a genuine resolution, not just surface retraction.

**Assessment rationale:** Self-correction criterion is satisfied, though triggered by one scaffolding prompt rather than purely independently. The student's recovery reasoning was substantive. First-exposure concept with one Level 4 self-correction and no Level 5 or 6 independent moments. Per calibration anchors, this places the score at the lower end of the first-exposure range (0.30–0.40), pulled slightly upward by the quality of the self-correction reasoning.

</concept_analysis>

---

Now addressing the synthesis moment at the end of the session, which touched on cross-concept integration rather than any single concept from the graph. For completeness, I note:

The student attempted to connect digital signatures and mining via "the principle of asymmetry" — Level 5/6 attempt. The analogy was structurally incorrect (the two asymmetries are different mechanisms), the Companion corrected it, the student then proposed "non-determinism" as the common effect but could not develop it and abandoned it ("ah sei lá. chega"). This is a failed Level 6 attempt — the student was reaching for generative connection but could not complete it. This is not penalized as a misconception (the student made the attempt and recognized they couldn't complete it), but it does not count as a positive evidence moment for either concept.

digital_signatures.evidence_summary: Multiple Level 5 independent moments (efficiency rationale, confidentiality limitation) and one solid procedural reconstruction after minimal scaffolding, producing an improving trajectory across the session. A weak persisting misconception about the internal verification mechanics (what exactly is 'decrypted' in the comparison step) was partially addressed but not self-corrected by the student.
digital_signatures.source_notes: Arrived at the session with prior asymmetric-cryptography knowledge that let them produce a near-complete description unprompted. Correctly identified the efficiency rationale for signing the hash (not the message) and independently stated that signatures don't guarantee confidentiality. Verification mechanics are slightly muddled — described 'decrypting the hash of the transaction' rather than the cleaner model of decrypting the signature to recover the signer's hash, then independently hashing the received message and comparing. Next session should ask the student to walk through verification step by step without scaffolding, specifically probing what the two objects being compared are and where each comes from.

proof_of_work.evidence_summary: Consistent Level 5 independent responses throughout: correctly described brute-force nonce search, identified the combinatorial difficulty, characterized the attacker's required effort, and grounded security in economic cost — all without scaffolding. One minor inversion ('asymmetry makes validation hard') was corrected by the Companion and not self-corrected, but surrounding evidence suggests it was a phrasing error rather than a structural misconception.
proof_of_work.source_notes: Correctly and independently described PoW as brute-force nonce search, identified that finding is expensive while verifying is trivial, and grounded the security argument in economic cost without prompting. Did not demonstrate the difficulty-adjustment mechanism or the 51% attack framing in this session. Next session should ask the student to explain what happens to the leading-zeros target as the network's computational power grows, to test whether the difficulty-adjustment concept is accessible from their current model.

mining.evidence_summary: Persistent misconception about the nonce's role (described as 'giving access' or 'granting rights' to block data) required two corrections and multiple scaffolding steps to resolve procedurally. The procedural mechanics (what validators calculate, what the hash target looks like) were reached only through scaffolding. Contrasting strength: the incentive and game-theory reasoning at the end was Level 5 independent, with the game-theory invocation being the student's own connection.
mining.source_notes: Has the incentive logic (economic cost, game-theoretic framing) firmly in hand — invoked game theory independently and correctly. Procedural mechanics are shaky: described the nonce as 'giving access to block data' twice before being corrected, and needed step-by-step scaffolding to correctly describe what validators do with the provided nonce. Next session should ask the student to describe what a miner does step by step — specifically what the nonce is, what it is combined with, and what condition the resulting hash must satisfy — before any new material.

bitcoin_as_case_study.evidence_summary: Initial incorrect reasoning (PoW necessary even in permissioned chains) was reversed after one scaffolding question, with the student completing the correct reasoning independently — articulating that known identities make PoW unnecessary and that other audit mechanisms suffice. Self-correction was substantive, not just answer replacement, but was triggered by a single prompt rather than arising unprompted.
bitcoin_as_case_study.source_notes: Initially imported PoW-as-always-necessary framing before recognizing the identity/accountability distinction with one prompt. Once oriented, correctly articulated why PoW is unnecessary in permissioned contexts (known identities, auditability, alternative consensus mechanisms). Next session should present a new scenario — e.g., a consortium of banks vs. a public DeFi protocol — and ask the student to reason from scratch which design choices are appropriate and why, without any scaffolding.

cryptography_introduction.evidence_summary: Student showed genuine Level 5 independence early (encryption definition, hash/encryption distinction), then regressed into a key-as-hash misconception requiring multiple scaffolding steps to resolve, closing with passive acknowledgment. Trajectory was declining after the early peak — the session probed deeper and the student's independent understanding did not hold.
cryptography_introduction.source_notes: Can articulate what encryption does (transforms text irreversibly-for-outsiders) and correctly distinguishes it from hashing, but formed a confused model of what a key *is* — twice described the key as a hash, and could only abandon this framing through scaffolding, not through independent reasoning. Next session should ask the student to define 'chave criptográfica' in their own words before any new material, specifically probing whether they can separate the key (parameter) from the transformation (algorithm) without prompting.

symmetric_cryptography.evidence_summary: Only one substantive engagement — a Level 1 echo of the Companion's definition of 'simétrica.' Student explicitly could not engage with the key-distribution problem. No independent reasoning demonstrated on this concept.
symmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Symmetric cryptography was introduced and immediately defined by the Companion; student echoed the definition without demonstrating understanding. Could not engage with the key-exchange problem at all. Next session should ask the student to explain what symmetric cryptography is and why it has a fundamental limitation, before any scaffolding — this will reveal whether the echo-level exposure produced any retention.

byzantine_generals_problem.evidence_summary: Student produced two Level 5 responses without scaffolding: independently arrived at the majority-threshold intuition, and invoked the 2/3 threshold by name before the Companion stated it. Also independently identified financial incentive as the motive for Byzantine behavior. One passive acknowledgment moment mid-session was the weakest point. Trajectory improved across the session.
byzantine_generals_problem.source_notes: Independently invoked the 2/3 threshold and identified financial incentives as the driver of dishonest behavior — both unprompted. Did not demonstrate the procedural mechanics (how a BFT algorithm actually produces consensus across rounds), and the 1/3 fault tolerance limit was supplied by the Companion rather than derived by the student. Next session should ask the student to work through a four-node example with one traitor to test whether the threshold reasoning transfers to procedure.

hash_functions.evidence_summary: One Level 5 moment early (independent correct statement that hashes cannot be reversed), with no further coverage of the concept in this session. No opportunity to assess depth, mechanism, or properties beyond directionality.
hash_functions.source_notes: Correctly and independently stated that hashes are irreversible, which is an improvement over the prior sessions where this was confused. However, prior profile notes the student cannot explain *why* hashes are irreversible — that gap was not addressed this session. Next session should open by asking the student to explain what a hash function does and why it cannot be reversed, before introducing any new cryptography material.

consensus_mechanisms.evidence_summary: Student could not arrive at PoW or PoS independently (Level 2 early, required the Companion to introduce both). After introduction, demonstrated Level 5 reasoning: correctly connected fraud attempts to economic loss across both mechanisms, and independently mapped PoW vs. PoS trade-offs including the PoS centralization concern. Trajectory improved from scaffolded start to independent analysis after concept introduction.
consensus_mechanisms.source_notes: Could not generate the PoW/PoS mechanisms without the Companion introducing them, but once introduced, correctly identified the PoS centralization-of-power concern independently — a non-trivial insight. Summary of PoS as 'mais rede, mais poder' slightly imprecise (stake, not network size). Next session should test whether the student can name and explain both mechanisms from scratch, and ask them to construct a scenario where PoS centralization creates a specific problem.

information_security_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Level 5 on integrity, authentication, and confidentiality (all independent, all unprompted); Level 6 on the self-generated hash-dependency model; Level 3 recovery on hash verification after an incorrect inversion assumption. Trajectory improved from one scaffolded moment to consistent independence. Weak misconception about hash invertibility was functionally resolved but not theoretically explained.
information_security_fundamentals.source_notes: Independently named all three pillars of the CIA triad and described digital signatures correctly. Constructed an original conceptual model of hash-like character dependency. Does not understand *why* hash functions are one-directional — accepted the correction procedurally and applied it correctly in the blockchain section, but could not explain the theoretical basis. Next session should open by asking the student to explain why hashes cannot be reversed.

blockchain_trilemma.evidence_summary: One strong Level 5 moment (independently traced scalability → synchronization risk → security risk), followed by a weak persisting misconception (decentralization reduces security via reversibility framing), followed by Level 2 scaffolded recovery. Trajectory mixed; the misconception was corrected by the Companion but not resolved by the student.
blockchain_trilemma.source_notes: Conflated decentralization with reduced security by importing the reversibility framing from prior sessions — stated 'descentralização = falta de segurança,' which inverts the correct relationship. Companion corrected this, but the student's recovery was a scaffolded echo. Can trace the scalability-security tension independently but does not yet have a stable model of where decentralization sits in the trilemma. Next session should ask the student to explain the trilemma in their own words before any scaffolding, specifically probing the decentralization-scalability (not security) link.

game_theory_and_trust.evidence_summary: Student produced Level 5 reasoning on detection probability independently, reached penalty logic after one scaffolded prompt, and generated a compressed but accurate synthesis ('o desonesto sai no prejuízo') that qualifies as Level 6 — their own formulation of the rational-choice incentive structure. Trajectory improved; the generative closing synthesis is the strongest signal.
game_theory_and_trust.source_notes: Student synthesized the game-theoretic incentive logic independently as 'o desonesto sai no prejuízo' — a correct compressed formulation that was their own, not the Companion's phrasing. Reasoning about penalty was only partially arrived at independently (required one scaffolding prompt to reach the economic cost angle). Next session should probe whether the student can articulate the distinction between 'hard to get away with' (detection) and 'not worth trying' (economic penalty) as separate mechanisms, since the session showed the student defaults to the detection framing first.

blockchain_types.evidence_summary: Level 5 on public/private distinctions and the absence of proof-of-work in permissioned chains; Level 6 on the hybrid model — student independently generated the central bank digital currency use case (Real Digital), which the Companion confirmed as accurate. blockchain_types.source_notes: Generated the CBDC use case for consortium blockchain independently and accurately. asymmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Can explain the symmetric/asymmetric distinction and the key-distribution solution independently and in own words.
